0: Whetztone — the operational manual for ZAND, the pure-digit pscale primitive. Each branch answers an operational question an LLM caller will ask: what is the signature, how is the address parsed and canonicalised, how is the shape determined, what does write do, how does star work, what substrate am I on. Reading the whole block teaches everything about calling zand; reading one branch answers one question; reading a sub-entry answers one sub-question. A zand call into whetztone returns exactly the slice the calling context needed.

1.0: Signature. zand(block, number, attention, content?, star?, block_loader?). The first three positionals locate WHAT is being addressed (block dict and pscale coordinates). The optional content turns the call from read to write. The optional star turns leaf-string references into recursive zand calls. The optional block_loader is required only when star is true.
1.1: block — the JSON tree itself, a Python dict (or equivalent in the host language). No wrapper, no metadata field, no tuning object — the dict IS the block. Substrate is responsible for fetching this dict from storage.
1.2: number — a pscale address, string or numeric. At most one decimal point. Dotted form preserves under supernesting; bare form walks literally. See branch 2 for canonicalisation.
1.3: attention — a pscale integer, or None. When None, the call returns a spindle (chain of zero-texts root-to-terminus). When set, attention combined with terminus pscale determines the shape — see branch 3.
1.4: content — the payload for a write. Omit for read. Shape must match what a read at (number, attention) would return: a string for point, a digit-keyed object for ring or directory, a list of address-text entries for disc.
1.5: star — a boolean controlling reference resolution. When true, leaf strings matching `blockname` or `blockname:address` are resolved by recursive zand calls. Default false; the caller opts in.
1.6: block_loader — a callable name -> dict (or None if absent). Encapsulates substrate routing — Postgres, federated beach, filesystem, etc. Required only when star is true.

2.0: Address parsing. A pscale address is a number with at most one decimal point. The parser splits the address into left-of-decimal and right-of-decimal digit lists. The floor is ALWAYS discovered first by walking the root zero-spine. Floor-aware padding applies to dotted addresses; bare addresses are walked literally.

1.0: The decimal anchor. At most one decimal point per address. The decimal sits between pscale 0 (the rightmost left-digit) and pscale -1 (the first right-digit). It marks the floor boundary so a human reader can tell at a glance which digits are at-floor and which below.
1.1: For a floor 1 block: '1.5' walks 1,5 — digit 1 at the floor, digit 5 one below.
1.2: For a floor 2 block: '23.4' walks 2,3,4 — digit 2 above floor, digit 3 at floor, digit 4 below.

2.0: Floor discovery. To determine a block's floor, walk the zero-spine from root: descend digit 0 while the value is an object, until reaching a string. The number of zero-steps is the floor depth. floor_depth(block) does this for any block; no metadata field is required.
2.1: Floor 1: root[0] is a string directly.
2.2: Floor 2: root[0][0] is a string. The block has supernested once; the original root content is reachable via two zero-key descents.
2.3: Floor 0: root has no [0] key at all. The block is unanchored — rare but legitimate, parsed without floor reasoning.

3.0: Floor-aware pinning. Every address pins to the floor via a decimal point — explicit (dotted) or implicit (bare). The parser ALWAYS checks the floor first by walking the root zero-spine, then canonicalises the input. The decimal placement IS the floor boundary in every case. Empty input (the empty string '' or None) is also a valid address: it represents the root with no walk; a spindle call returns just the root entry; a disc call with attention set returns the cross-section at that depth.
3.1: Dotted addresses are floor-anchored at write-time. The explicit decimal fixes the floor for this address. If the current floor is deeper than the address's floor (post-supernest), the parser left-pads with zeros to match — preserving the original semantic position across growth. Worked example: '30.5' written at floor 2 walks 3,0,5. After supernesting to floor 3, '30.5' parses with padding: left becomes 0,3,0; walk becomes 0,3,0,5 — one zero-step deeper into the now-extended zero-spine, then the same 3,0,5 walk as before. Same semantic position; same terminus pscale.
3.2: Bare addresses are floor-pinned at read-time. The implicit decimal sits at the current floor position. Bare with length equal-or-longer than floor: split at the floor boundary (left = first floor digits, right = remainder). Bare with length shorter than floor: left-pad with zeros to reach floor. Worked examples on floor 2: '305' walks 3,0,5 (split: '30.5'); '35' walks 3,5 (split: '35'); '3' walks 0,3 (pad: '3' as canonical display).
3.3: Dotted addresses with left-of-decimal exceeding the current floor are rejected. The decimal anchors at the floor; nothing above the floor for those digits to walk through.
3.4: Bare re-pins each read; dotted does not. Use dotted addresses for durable semantic references that must survive supernesting. Use bare for current-floor convenience — quick writes, one-shot probes, addresses that mean 'here at this floor'.

4.0: Canonical address form. The canonical display form always has a single decimal point (or none if the walk fits at-or-above the floor). Leading zeros above the floor are stripped for display; the parser will re-pad from floor on re-read, preserving the walk.
4.1: Walk notation. When discussing the digit sequence the walker traverses, use commas: 3,0,5 — not dots. The address has one dot (30.5); the walk has commas (3,0,5). Multi-dot forms (3.0.5) are pre-zand legacy and read as a structural hazard.
4.2: Worked examples of canonical display, all on a floor-3 block: '0023' walks 0,0,2,3 and displays as '2.3' (above-floor 002 strips its leading zeros to '2', then dot, then '3' below floor). '1000' walks 1,0,0,0 and displays as '100.0' (above-floor 100 keeps all three digits since they sit at-or-above floor, then dot, then '0' below). '1.' walks 0,0,1 and displays as '1' (the walk fits entirely at-or-above floor — no decimal in display). '305' walks 3,0,5 and displays as '305' (same reason). The rule: format the above-floor portion with leading zeros stripped (preserving at least one digit), then add '.' + the below-floor portion only if the below-floor portion is non-empty.
4.3: Returned `address` field. Spindle, point, ring, and directory results carry an `address` field with the canonical display form. Callers can round-trip: read the address from a returned spindle, pass it back into zand on the same block, get the same walk. The address field is the protocol-level identifier of the position — what one process tells another to refer to a point in shared space.

5.0: Validation. Errors in address syntax surface explicitly with InvalidAddressError. Addresses are never silently truncated, reinterpreted, or rewritten.
5.2: Non-digit characters raise InvalidAddressError. An address must consist only of digits 0-9 and at most one decimal period.
5.3: Dotted addresses with left-of-decimal exceeding the floor raise InvalidAddressError.
5.4: A write to a malformed address may still land somewhere — just not where the writer intended. Defensive practice: read back after writing to confirm.

3.0: Shape derivation. The shape that zand returns is determined entirely by two pscale values: the terminus pscale (computed as floor minus walk-length) and the attention pscale. The caller does not pick a mode; the (number, attention) pair determines the shape.

1.0: Walk-with-empty semantics. The walk NEVER halts on missing structure. It walks every digit of the canonical address. Each step produces a spindle entry tagged with status: voiced (position carries a string), headless (object with no zero-text), or absent (no node here). Pscale arithmetic matches the address by construction.
1.1: voiced — the position has a string at its 0-position (directly or via zero-spine descent). The text field carries that string.
1.2: headless — the position exists as an object but has no zero-text. The author has not yet voiced this position. Headless entries are first-class TODOs the author can fill in later.
1.3: absent — no JSON node at this position. The path fell off the tree. Once absent, all deeper entries are also absent.

2.0: Point. When attention equals the terminus pscale, the call returns a point — a single text at the spindle's terminus. The point carries pscale, text, and status.
2.1: Worked example. On a floor 1 block, address '1.5' walks 1,5, terminus pscale = 1 - 2 = -1. zand(block, '1.5', attention=-1) returns a point: the string at block[1][5] (or null with status absent if the position doesn't exist).
2.2: Use a point when you need exactly one piece of content and have already decided which one. Returns {mode: 'point', pscale: -1, text: '...', status: 'voiced'}.

3.0: Ring. When attention is shallower than terminus (higher pscale integer), the call returns a ring — the set of siblings at attention's level, along the spindle's path. The ring's parent is the spindle's position at (attention_depth - 1); siblings are that parent's digit children.
3.1: Worked example. On a floor 1 block, address '2.3' walks 2,3, terminus pscale -1. zand(block, '2.3', attention=0) returns a ring at pscale 0 (depth 1, root's children). The ring shows block's digit children: 0, 1, 2, 5 (whatever digits root has). The walked sibling at this level is '2', flagged is_walked=true.
3.2: Use a ring to see lateral alternatives — what the writer offered at this level. Returns {mode: 'ring', pscale: 0, siblings: [{digit, text, status, is_branch, is_walked}, ...]}.
3.3: Voicing-aware ring. When a sibling is itself a branch (object), the ring entry shows is_branch=true and the text is the zero-text collected from that sibling. The caller sees the semantic of each branch, NEVER its digit children. This is structural hiding without a special character: positions 0,1 and 0,2 (children of the 0-voicing branch) are not enumerated alongside 1, 2, 3 (top-level digit children). The hidden sub-positions are reachable only by explicit walk.

4.0: Directory. When attention is deeper than terminus (lower pscale integer), the call returns a directory — the subtree rooted at the spindle's terminus, truncated to attention's depth. The directory answers 'what are my options below from where I am standing?'
4.1: Worked example. On a floor 1 block, address '2' walks just to depth 1, terminus pscale 0. zand(block, '2', attention=-1) returns a directory: the subtree at block[2], descending one level. Returns {mode: 'directory', subtree: {...}}.
4.2: Use a directory when you want the nested choice space below the spindle's terminus — all options at all sub-depths. Directory depth one returns a sub-tree carrying one ring; depth two returns a stack of rings.
4.3: Boundary truncation. When an object sits AT attention's depth — the leaves of the directory subtree — it is collapsed to its zero-text. Its digit children are not included. This keeps the subtree size proportional to the depth requested rather than expanding into the full descendant tree. Worked example on a floor-1 block {0: 'root', 1: {0: 'A', 2: {0: 'A2', 3: 'A23'}}}: zand(block, '1', attention=-1) returns directory subtree {0: 'A', 2: 'A2'} — the '2' subbranch is collapsed to its zero-text 'A2'; its child '23' is omitted because it sits beyond attention's depth (at pscale -2).

5.0: Disc. When the number is empty and attention is set, the call returns a disc — all positions at attention's depth across the entire block, regardless of angular path. Unrooted; a transversal cross-section.
5.1: Worked example. On a floor 1 block, zand(block, number=None, attention=-1) returns a disc: a list of all positions at pscale -1 across the whole block, each with its canonical address and text. Returns {mode: 'disc', pscale: -1, nodes: [{address, text, status}, ...]}.
5.2: Use a disc to survey the block at one scale — what content lives at this depth, irrespective of where in the angular structure.
5.3: Emit-every-position. The disc emits every position at the target depth, including digit-0 children that hold strings serving as voicings of their parent branches. Every position is first-class. Worked example on a floor-1 block {0: 'X', 1: 'A', 3: 'C'}: a disc at pscale 0 (depth 1) emits THREE positions — address '0' (text='X'), '1' (text='A'), '3' (text='C'). The '0' position is not suppressed by its voicing role. (The same 'X' is also reachable as the block's root zero-text — but in the disc it is a position in its own right at depth 1.) Floor-3 corollary: positions like root[1][2][0] (a voicing string at depth 3) appear in the disc at pscale 0 alongside digit-walked positions like root[1][2][3].

6.0: Rooted versus unrooted. Point, ring, and directory all sit along the spindle — they describe the agent's local situation. Disc has no root — it describes the block's global structure at one scale. An agent reading 'I am at X; what are my options?' asks for point/ring/directory. An agent reading 'forget where I am — what exists at this scale anywhere?' asks for disc.
6.1: Spindle as the default. When only number is given (no attention), the call returns a spindle — the chain of zero-texts root-to-terminus, each tagged with pscale and status. This is the contextualised narrowing the writer constructed; what an agent typically starts with before deciding what shape to request next.

4.0: Write. When content is provided, zand writes instead of reading. The shape of the write is the same shape that a read at the same (number, attention) would return. Lenient by default: missing intermediates are created as headless objects; intermediate strings are preserved as the new object's 0-voicing. Four write modes exist: point-write, ring-write, directory-write, whole-write — each is the conjugate of its read counterpart. Every successful write returns {mode: '<shape>-write', ok: true, address: '<canonical>'}; failures return {mode: 'error', ok: false, error: '<message>'} or raise InvalidAddressError if the address is malformed. The address field on writes is the canonical display form of the position the write landed at.

1.0: Point write. Replace a single string at the spindle's terminus. Content is a string. The walker navigates to the parent of the terminus (creating intermediate objects as needed) and sets the terminus key to the content.
1.1: Worked example. zand(block, '1.5', attention=-1, content='new leaf') sets block[1][5] = 'new leaf'.
1.2: Deep write — the keystone semantic. Writing to an address whose intermediate structure does not yet exist auto-creates the path. Intermediate positions that were strings are LIFTED to objects with the original string preserved as the new object's 0-voicing. Example: writing 'deep' at '1.234' on {0: 'root', 1: 'branch one'} produces {0: 'root', 1: {0: 'branch one', 2: {3: {4: 'deep'}}}}. The original meaning of '1' is not lost — it is preserved as the 0-voicing of the new object. Positions 2 and 3 are headless intermediates (TODOs for later voicing).

2.0: Ring write — conjugate of ring read. Replace digit-keyed children at the parent at depth (attention_depth − 1) along the spindle. Content is a digit-keyed dict; the existing digit children at that depth are removed and the content's digit children take their place. The ring's parent is the same JSON node the read-side ring would have enumerated from.
2.1: Worked example. zand(block, '1.5', attention=0, content={'0': 'voice', '1': 'new', '5': 'replaced'}) on a floor-1 block: attention=0 means the ring sits at depth 1 (the floor); parent_depth = 0; parent = block (root). The write replaces root's digit children with the given entries. Read-side `zand(block, '1.5', attention=0)` would enumerate the same node's children — same parent, same semantics.
2.2: Walk semantics on ring-write. The walk's digits past parent_depth are IGNORED by the write itself. The walk only navigates the prefix [0:parent_depth]; the remaining digits exist in the spindle but do not affect where the write lands. Example: '1.5' with attention=0 has walk [1,5] but the write goes to root's children (parent_depth=0), so the '1' and '5' digits are unused for navigation. The walk's role is purely spindle-side context; the write follows attention's depth rule strictly.

3.0: Directory write. Replace the entire subtree at the spindle's terminus. Content is a dict — the new subtree. The existing subtree is discarded; the new subtree takes its place. This is how a whole sub-block is inserted in a single call.
3.1: Worked example. zand(block, '1', attention=-3, content={'0': 'X', '2': {'0': 'Y', '3': 'Z'}}) replaces block[1] with the nested object given.

4.0: Whole-block write. When number is None (or empty), attention is None, and content is a dict, zand performs a whole-block write — clears the block's existing keys and installs content's keys, MUTATING THE BLOCK DICT IN PLACE to preserve dict identity for callers holding references. The floor may change as a result of the write if the new content's zero-spine has different depth. Use whole-write to replace a block atomically from a single call.
4.1: Worked example. zand(block, content={'0': 'new root voicing', '1': 'new leaf one'}) on any prior block — block's existing keys are cleared and replaced with the new {0, 1} pair. After the write, floor=1 (the new content's zero-spine is one level deep).

5.0: Verification. After any write, defensive practice is to read back at the same address to confirm. A malformed address may have landed the content elsewhere; the read-back catches that immediately.
5.1: Atomicity is at the single-block boundary. A single zand call is atomic within one block; multi-block transactions are not guaranteed. For compositions that cross blocks, the caller manages consistency.

5.0: Star — reference resolution. By default, leaf strings are returned verbatim. When the caller passes star=true, a leaf matching `blockname`, `blockname:address`, or `blockname:address:attention` is recognised as a deferred zand call and resolved via block_loader. Whatever coordinates the reference carries are passed to the recursive call; the shape derives normally. Star is the call-resolution operator.

1.0: Reference syntax. A leaf is recognised as a reference when it matches one of three forms: `name`, `name:address`, or `name:address:attention`. Formal grammar: name = letter (letter | digit | underscore | hyphen)* (colon-namespacing allowed: name = name (':' name)*); address = digit+ ('.' digit*)?; attention = '-'? digit+. The colons inside a name are followed by another letter-led segment; the colon before address/attention is followed by digits (or a minus then digits). This disambiguation rule fully parses cases like 'sed:commons:13:-1' → name='sed:commons', address='13', attention=-1 (the '13' is digit-led so it can't extend the name).
1.1: Examples of recognised references: 'sunztone' (whole block), 'sunztone:5' (spindle to 5), 'sunztone:5:0' (point at 5), 'sunztone:5.1:-3' (directory from 5,1 down to pscale -3), 'sed:commons:13:1' (ring at pscale 1 around sed:commons position 13).
1.2: Examples returned verbatim (not references) even with star=true: 'this is just text', 'see also Smith 2024', '123' (must start with letter). Leaves that don't match the reference regex are plain content.

2.0: Resolution. When star=true and a recognised reference is encountered, the walker calls zand recursively on the named block with the reference's address and attention. The shape that returns is whatever zand normally returns for (address, attention) against the target block's floor: whole (no address), spindle (address, no attention), point (attention = terminus), ring (attention shallower), directory (attention deeper).
2.1: Recursion. A resolved reference may itself contain references; with star=true on the outer call, nested references are resolved as well. Star propagates: the recursive zand call carries star=true (and the same block_loader), so leaves of the inner result that look like references resolve too. Three levels of star propagation (A→B→C) all resolve in a single outer call if star=true. The caller's Python call stack bounds the recursion; there is no built-in depth limit. Cycles (A references B references A) will recurse until stack overflow — callers controlling federation should rely on agent-level cycle detection if cycles are a risk.
2.2: Failure handling. If the named block is not loadable, the reference is returned verbatim — the caller sees the unresolved text and decides what to do. No exception is raised; the reference simply does not resolve.

3.0: Block name as function. Every block name is a function signature; the internal structure is the body. A zand call with the block name and address is the invocation. Networks of star-linked named blocks ARE programs walked by an LLM. Walking is thinking — static structure plus live walk is reasoning-as-traversal.
3.1: Opt-in design. Star is opt-in at every call. The caller decides whether following references is desirable in this context. Debugging might want references verbatim; resolved presentation wants them followed.
3.2: Self-reference is allowed and useful. A block whose leaf references itself folds the walk into a control loop — the 0-text as reference signal, the digit siblings as perceptual signal, the gap as error driving action.

6.0: Substrate. The block argument to zand is always a JSON dict in memory; how it got there is the substrate layer's responsibility. The substrate sits below the function. A block_loader passed for star resolution encapsulates the substrate routing. The function is invariant under substrate; any backend implementing the loader interface can serve zand.

1.0: Block name as substrate router. The block name's prefix tells the substrate where the block lives. The zand call shape is invariant — same signature for any substrate; only the loader differs.
1.1: Ordinary names route to the default beach. A name with no recognised prefix is a public commons block. Reads free; writes locked by opt-in passphrase.
1.2: sed:<collective> routes to a sedimentary collective — append-only registry of position-locked declarations.
1.3: grain:<pair_id> routes to a bilateral channel — encrypted between two named agents.
1.4: https://<origin> routes to a federated beach hosted at that URL, via /.well-known/pscale-beach. Federation is opt-in by site.

2.0: Adapter interface. The minimum interface a substrate must provide is the block_loader callable — name in, block dict out (or None). For writes, the substrate must additionally provide save — name and block in, success or conflict out. Locks, secrets, and substrate-specific allocation live within the adapter.
2.1: load_block(name) — returns block as a dict, or None if absent. Required for reads and for star resolution.
2.2: save_block(name, dict, version_token?) — writes the block, optionally with optimistic-concurrency token. Required for writes.
2.3: Additional methods (lock state, position allocation, sed: and grain: specifics) depend on which substrates the adapter serves. Above the adapter, zand sees only Python dicts.

3.0: Modifiers. Four orthogonal access axes wrap zand without altering the function's geometry — face, tier, secret, gray. Each is checked by the substrate before zand is called; failed checks reject the call without revealing block contents.
3.1: face — CADO (Character, Author, Designer, Observer). Which role the caller wears determines what kinds of operations are permitted. Validated against sed: collective memberships; observer requires no membership.
3.2: tier — SMH (Soft, Medium, Hard). Which tier the call runs at determines the aperture within whatever the face permits.
3.3: secret — write-lock proof. Required on locked blocks for any write; verified by the substrate server-side.
3.4: gray — opt-in content encryption. On unlocked ordinary blocks, content can be self-encrypted with the writer's secret as key. The structure remains visible; content remains private.
